course_description:
Gender and Political Leadership in Australia

How do ideas about gender shape Australia's political landscape?

This course examines how gender norms influence political leadership, political representation and the issues that dominate Australia's political agenda.

It explores how gender can play a role in who becomes a political leader, how leadership is performed, how leaders are represented in the media, and how they are judged by the public. It also considers how gender politics plays a role in major policy debates, from the gender pay gap, to family and domestic violence, to economic policy.

The course provides students with the skills and knowledge to undertake critical and intersectional gender analyses of Australian politics. Students will consider how gender politics shapes the experiences of individual leaders, the attitudes of the electorate and the issues that they care about. It will provide valuable grounding for students interested in Australian politics, political history, public policy, legislative change, gender studies and leadership.

The course is co-taught by the Honorable Professor Annastacia Palaszczuk, AC, who brings first-hand experience of political leadership as Australia¿s longest-serving female premier, offering students a unique practitioner perspective on gender and leadership in Australian politics.

course_contacts.name: Dr Molly Murphy
course_contacts.notes: Dr Murphy is the primary contact for this course. Please contact Dr Murphy for all administrative and assessment-related queries.

UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims:
This course provides students with an in-depth understanding of a contemporary political issue. It seeks to guide students through the different debates, history, policy and political implications of this issue. In particular, it aims to:

I. To introduce students to a significant contemporary area of debate in Political Science

II. To enable students to link contemporary debates in Political Science with political practice in areas of governance, public policy, and community engagement

III. To encourage students to critically reflect upon contemporary political debates and identify the political and theoretical commitments of a range of perspectives.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and critically evaluate the assumptions that drive debates on contemporary political issues.
Contextualise contemporary political debates in relation to relevant theories of political science.
Situate contemporary political debates in the relevant histories of public policy.
Apply the necessary tools and skills for critical analysis in reading, textual analysis and writing.
Collaborate in groups, to speak and write persuasively and in an evidence-based manner about a range of political issues.

assessment_details.title: Inclass Activities
assessment_details.due_date: This item is due at the end of each class for Weeks 1-7 and 9-11. Time will be allocated for students to submit their work.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This course has ten weeks where in-class group activities (with 3-4 students in each group) will be conducted (Weeks 1-7; 9-11). Students will be given time to submit their group's activity at the end of the class. These will be graded out of 3 with each member of the group awarded the same grade. Your top 5 highest individual grades will count towards the 15% mark. If you fail to complete five, you will be marked accordingly. If problematic group work is observed, individual marks will be awarded for that week. Marking rubrics are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. This task is completed in person and has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Please include each group member's name on the submission. Each student must upload their submission to Blackboard.
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin .
In uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission. Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you don’t see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
This task is not eligible for extensions or deferrals. If you have a relevant SAP, please contact Dr Murphy at the start of semester to arrange an alternative assessment regime.

assessment_details.title: Group Presentation (Ministerial-level briefing)
assessment_details.due_date: 14/09/2026 - 18/09/2026 Presentations will occur in Week 8, either in class or by appointment, depending on class size.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Your group will be required to provide a ten-minute in-person Ministerial-level Briefing in Week 8. In your group, you will identify a key issue in Australian state or federal politics where gender inequality is a significant theme. This may be an existing policy issue (such as childcare subsidies), it may be an emerging political issue (for example need to change community’s views on family and domestic violence), or it may be an issue relevant to a political party (such as an argument for gender quotas in a political party). Your group must: Form groups of between 3-5 students, in Week 4 Determine who the briefing is for (eg: a relevant federal/state minister, the Premier, the Opposition Leader, etc) Clearly explain the issue, its urgency, who is impacted by this and in what way. Include relevant data where appropriate Provide a gender-responsive analysis of the issue Provide advice to the authority. You might wish to consider: What the policy response should be What the strengths, weaknesses, constraints and opportunities are in addressing the issue in this way What the timeline and key milestones are for this plan What the measures of success are Your presentation should be supported by powerpoint. Your group should then be prepared to answer 5 minutes of questions. You will be assessed as a group on your presentation and your response to questions. You will also receive an individual mark based on peer-assessment of your contribution to the group. Students will be asked to form groups in the tutorial in Week 4, complete a peer-assessment check-in (via Buddycheck) in Week 6, and a final peer-assessment review (via Buddycheck) by Friday 5pm of Week 8, after the presentations. This final peer assessment will count towards an individual grade component of the task. Failure to participate in the peer-assessment will result in a mark of 0 for the individual peer-marked component of the task. What you need to submit: Group Powerpoint slide submitted by Monday 1pm of Week 8 Peer-assessment (Buddycheck) by Friday 5pm Week 6 Peer-assessment (Buddycheck) by Friday 5pm, Week 8 (after the presentation) Marking rubrics and percentage weightings are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Activity Writeup
assessment_details.due_date: 27/07/2026 - 12/10/2026 You may submit this assessment at any time during the semester after the relevant activity has taken place. Submissions will be returned within two weeks. The last submission date in Monday 12 October 1pm (Week 11).
assessment_details.task_description: Choose one of the activities undertaken in class during the course. Write a 1,000 word applied paper on the activity. The structure of this submission is flexible and can be shaped by the activity. You must: Include a photo/attachment of the final output of the group for the relevant task (not counted in word count) Provide a mini literature review on this topic, both scholarly and, if appropriate, policy/ media/ speeches, etc (appr. 200-300 words) Include a reference list (not counted in word count). Expand on the original in-class task if needed Reflect on the approach, debates, and reasoning of the group – and any points at which you personally deviated from the group’s decision-making (300-400 words) Identify the likely strengths, weaknesses, gaps, barriers, constraints or risks of your group’s approach (as appropriate) (200-300 words) Marking rubrics are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin .
In uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission. Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you don’t see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Unless an extension is granted, penalties for late submission apply. Students are penalised 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item for every calendar day that an assessment item is late.
Marks will be deducted each day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point the submitted item will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24-hour block is triggered from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description: The final assessment for this course will be a central exam held during the central exam period. Students will have 2 hours to complete the exam. Students will be required to answer 2 questions in essay form. Students will be required to complete one question from Part A of the exam questions and the other from Part B. The topics covered in Part A correspond approximately with the first half of the course, while the topics covered in Part B correspond with the lectures in the second half of the course. Students will choose one of 6 possible questions from Part A and one of 6 possible questions from Part B. Each of the two questions will be worth 15 marks. The maximum score for the assessment will be 30 marks. Marking rubrics are available on the Blackboard site and discussed in Week One. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Week 01: Introduction: Why does gender matter in political leadership? Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 02: Masculinities, Femininities, and gender performativity in political leadership Learning outcomes: L02, L05
Week 03: The history of gender and political leadership in Australia Learning outcomes: L03, L05
Week 04: Gender equality public policy Students will form into groups for the Week 08 Ministerial Briefing Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Week 05: Gender Politics and the Media Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05
Week 06: Getting Elected: Elections, Parties, Quotas, and Campaigns Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Week 07: Gender and Indigenous Political Leadership Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05
Week 08: Ministerial Briefings This week is dedicated to the Ministerial Briefings. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Week 09: Intersectionality and political leadership Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05
Week 10: Gendered Parliamentary cultures and workplace Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05
Week 11: Gender and Voting Behaviour Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05
Week 12: The 2025 Federal Election and the 2026 Liberal Leadership Spill Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05
Week 13: Wrap-up and Exam Revision Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
